title: Record of Meeting: Summary of Discussions Among Members of the Community Depository Institutions Advisory Council
summary: The Community Depository Institutions Advisory Council met on April 11, 2024, providing qualitative assessments of regional economic conditions from depository institution representatives. Council members described the economy as "surprisingly resilient" but noted persistent inflationary pressures, with a bifurcation between upper- and lower-income households. Inflation has moderated from its peak but remains "stubbornly high," driven by strong consumer demand and elevated labor costs, though goods inflation is slowing while services inflation persists.
key_points: Inflation has moderated from peak but remains "stubbornly high," driven by both labor costs and strong consumer demand that continues to surprise to the upside
Goods inflation is slowing while services inflation persists due to associated labor costs; construction prices show net-zero change as rising labor costs offset decreasing raw material prices
Labor costs have moderated: pay increases averaged 6-7 percent last year and are expected to be closer to 4-5 percent this year
Housing prices have moderated but remain restrictive for most consumers; multifamily permit issuance has stalled in some Districts while remaining high in others
Non-skilled and blue-collar labor remains challenging to find, especially in manufacturing and trade sectors, while the talent pool for skilled white-collar workers has expanded
Council members expressed doubts about the near-term feasibility of the 2 percent inflation target in the current economic environment
why_it_matters: This CDIAC meeting summary provides ground-level economic intelligence from depository institutions across Federal Reserve Districts. While not official Fed policy, these qualitative reports offer insight into how regional economic conditions were being perceived by front-line financial institutions in April 2024, including the disconnect between goods and services inflation, labor market imbalances, and housing affordability challenges that continue to shape the macro environment.
macro_context: The document provides qualitative macro context from April 2024, indicating persistent inflation driven by services and labor costs, moderating goods inflation, cooling but still restrictive housing markets, and labor markets that remain tight for blue-collar workers while becoming more balanced for white-collar positions. This suggests a mixed macro picture with service-sector inflation remaining the primary concern for policy normalization.
important_numbers: 10 percent (technology cost increases in 2024 vs. prior 2-3 percent average)

issues_found: Source body is truncated; content beyond labor market discussion may be missing (housing discussion appears incomplete, other agenda items not visible)

human_review.notes: Minor issues are present but not significant enough to require human decision. The record is largely accurate and well-supported. Age of document (April 2024) and qualitative nature of advisory council input are appropriately noted.

notes: Historical record — published April 11, 2024, which is more than 90 days before TODAY (2026-04-12). Lowered confidence slightly.
